pattern: The fixed five-character format encodes two corner cells directly by position, so parsing out the two column letters and two row digits turns the problem into a simple nested range enumeration, column-major then row-minor.
hints: The input string always has the same five-character shape, so the column letters and row digits sit at fixed positions -- no general parsing is needed.
Once you have the starting and ending column letters and row numbers, the cells you need are exactly every combination of a column in that range with a row in that range.
Build the result by iterating columns as the outer loop and rows as the inner loop, since the required order lists all rows for one column before moving to the next column.

edge_inputs.description: a single row spanning several columns
a single cell, start equals end in both dimensions
a full single-column span across every row
the widest possible single-row column span
both dimensions span more than one value, exercising column-major then row-minor ordering
the largest possible rectangle under the constraints

stress.description: random valid column and row ranges
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    c1 = rng.randint(0, 25)
    c2 = rng.randint(c1, 25)
    r1 = rng.randint(1, 9)
    r2 = rng.randint(r1, 9)
    s = chr(65 + c1) + str(r1) + ':' + chr(65 + c2) + str(r2)
    return [s]

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def cellsInRange(self, s):
        left, right = s.split(':')
        c1, r1 = left[0], int(left[1])
        c2, r2 = right[0], int(right[1])
        cols = [chr(x) for x in range(ord(c1), ord(c2) + 1)]
        rows = list(range(r1, r2 + 1))
        return [col + str(row) for col in cols for row in rows]
